sop.coo_evaluation:
# Gaps — no SOP yet

- **Member complaints & incident escalation** – no documented pathway for handling complaints, injuries, safeguarding concerns, or disputes (critical for a women's-only space and duty of care).
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no SOP for cash/card handling, refunds, payment disputes, or monthly P&L sign-off.
- **Staff discipline & performance management** – no process for documenting underperformance, capability conversations, or termination.
- **Health & safety incident reporting** – no formal log or investigation template for accidents, near-misses, or equipment failures.
- **Member data protection & GDPR compliance** – no documented process for access requests, data deletion, or breach notification.
- **Retention/win-back campaigns** – no automated or manual process for re-engaging lapsed members or churn prevention.
- **Induction for new gym staff (non-coaches)** – assuming reception/admin staff exist; no onboarding SOP if they do.
- **Equipment maintenance log & fault reporting** – beyond facilities cleaning, no predictive or reactive maintenance schedule.

---

# Needs updating

- **Coaches SOP suite (17/17 built)** – likely missing: what to do when a coach is sick/absent (cover protocol), conflict resolution between coaches, how to handle a member complaint *about* a coach, de-escalation scripts.
- **Client onboarding** – check if it covers: payment failure handling, cancellation windows, refund policy, what happens if member is injured mid-contract.
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – Email Newsletter & Blog Post listed as not built; also missing: crisis comms (e.g., facility closure, coach departure, negative review response).
- **Facilities (4/4)** – probably missing: deep-clean schedule, COVID/illness protocol,

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing & AR/M drift is your biggest leak.** June AR/M crashed to £120.48 (down from £165.56 in May, down from £170.43 in Dec). You've added 25 net members since May (122→147) but revenue fell £2.5k. Either new joiners are on discount packages or existing members aren't upgrading. Audit the June cohort pricing immediately; run a 3–5% price rise on renewals and kill any introductory underpricing after month 3.
- **Churn spiked badly in April–May (8.33% and 5.74%) then recovered to 0% in June — capitalise on that momentum.** June's zero churn with 147 members is your strongest month. Identify what fixed the retention issue (new facility, staff change, programme?) and double down. That's your retention floor now.
- **Quiet class fill-rate is hidden.** You have 147 members but revenue/profit is weak relative to headcount. Analyse which classes run under 60% capacity and either consolidate them, move timings to peak demand, or introduce small-group training upsells (1-to-1 or 3-person sessions at £25–35/session). Target: convert 15% of members to one paid add-on per month by end of Q3.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people costs ballooned Jan–April (£8.2k→£30.5k) then normalised to £9.4k in June, but that's still 50% higher than Sep–Nov baseline (£4.3–4.9k).** Post-relocation, scrutinise utilities (larger space), supplies, and any new contracts (cleaning, maintenance, rent). June's £9.4k on £17.7k revenue (53% of sales) is acceptable for a new bigger studio, but audit the line-items: is there waste? Target: hold non-people expenses at ≤48% of revenue by August.
- **Payroll in June (£3.686k on £17.7k revenue = 20.8%) is creeping up and now exceeds the Sep–Nov average (£2.6–2.7k).** You've added staff for the bigger studio — justified — but track hours tightly. If the new team includes fixed contracts, confirm they scale with member growth. June's headcount should support £22–25k revenue; you're at £17.7k. Tighten scheduling to match demand peaks; aim for pay

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is overloaded**: 133 sessions is unsustainable (roughly 31 per week). Redistribute her Personal Training load—Eilis can take more 1:1s to ease pressure and develop her skillset, freeing JoJo for group classes where her technical expertise (Build: Upper, Circuits) is hardest to replace.

• **Grace's Monday-only constraint is creating a bottleneck in Build: Lower**: She ran 17 in June alone. Consider rotating Build: Lower across other days with Eilis or a new hire so it's not dependent on one person and one day—this improves member choice and resilience.

• **Eilis is under-used and under-challenged**: She's at 48 sessions (11 per week) with capacity to grow. Increase her Elevate, Plate, and Capacity class allocation on Wed/Thu/Fri, and assign her more 1:1 Personal Training—this builds her confidence and reduces JoJo's burden without breaking the Tuesday constraint.

• **Body Composition Scans are fragmented across all three coaches**: Consolidate these into one or two people's responsibility to create clear ownership, reduce scheduling friction, and free up coaching time for group classes where demand seems stronger.

• **Cover the No Sweat Intro gap**: Grace ran only 3 in June. Make this a scheduled weekly offering (assign to Eilis or rotate) to systematise onboarding and ensure new members aren't turned away or delayed.

• **Hire or cross-train urgently**: With JoJo at breaking point and Grace restricted to Mondays, you need a third strong group coach, especially for technical classes (Build: Upper, Circuits, Mobility).

class_stats.suggestion:
**1. Consolidate low-attendance classes.** Engine, Capacity, Barbelle, Strongevity, and Games Night are consistently underattended (12-52 attendees over 90 days). Consider removing these from the timetable or merging them into existing popular slots to free up instructor time and avoid scattered classes.

**2. Boost Monday and Saturday attendance.** These quietest days have 150+ fewer attendees than Wednesday. Add a second "Build: Upper" or "Build: Full Body" session on Monday evening and a Saturday morning slot to capture members who prefer weekends or early-week workouts.

**3. Shift Plate to a peak-time slot.** Plate shows growing interest (83 attendees in last 30 days vs. 226 over 90 days) but remains underutilized. Move it to Wednesday or Tuesday during prime hours to increase visibility and capture momentum from busier days.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

**Get the pricing right first, then build backwards:**
- Run a 20-minute survey with 10-15 active members asking: "What would you pay for X?" (1-to-1 coaching, class packs, nutrition add-ons). Price anchors to their willingness-to-pay, not your costs. This beats guessing.

**Create TWO separate documents (not one):**
- **Member-facing**: Clean, benefit-driven ("Get stronger in 12 weeks" not "12 sessions"). List tiers (e.g., "Unlimited Classes + 1 Monthly Coaching Session = £X"). Include what's *not* included to avoid refund requests.
- **Coach-facing**: Operating specs—which services coaches can sell, upsell rules, how to position each tier, commission/bonus structure if applicable. Coaches won't sell what confuses them.

**Test the menu with your top 3 coaches before launch:**
- Have them pitch it to a member in front of you. Watch where they stumble. That's where your language needs fixing. One awkward explanation = lost sales.

**Make it live in two places simultaneously:**
- On your website/booking system *and* printed at reception. Members see it when deciding; coaches reference it during conversations. No "I'm not sure what we offer" moments.

**Set a quarterly review date:**
- Document what sells, what doesn't. After 90 days, kill the duds and add what members are asking for. Live document, not a one-time project.

What's your current pricing structure looking like?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

**Audit your current drop-off points first** — Use Google Analytics to identify where visitors are bouncing hardest (homepage vs. pricing vs. membership tiers). Test a hypothesis: are they leaving because they don't understand *who* the gym is for, or because pricing/commitment questions aren't answered? That tells you what to fix.

**Create a "Women's-Only Gym for [Specific Type]" headline test** — Your current messaging likely says "women's fitness." Instead, test landing pages that lead with *who thrives there*: "For women 40+ returning to fitness," "For busy professionals who don't want gym politics," "For women training for strength." Whichever messaging gets the highest click-to-booking rate wins—use that across all pages.

**Build a rapid FAQ section using your actual member questions** — Spend 1 hour reviewing recent member inquiries (emails, calls, messages). List the top 8 questions. Add these as expandable FAQs above the fold on your landing page. Example: "Can I come if I'm completely new to lifting?" Place this *before* the CTA button so objection-holders convert instead of bouncing.

**A/B test one high-intent page element this week** — Pick either (1) your membership pricing display or (2) your "what you get" benefits list. Change one thing: reorder benefits by member value, simplify pricing tiers, or add social proof (member transformations). Run it for 5 days. Measure conversion rate shift.

**Strip unnecessary options from your funnel** — If you have 4+ membership tiers or multiple CTAs, you're causing decision paralysis.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Execute Member Feedback System for The Forge

• **Skip the form—use post-class capture instead.** Hand out a 3-question card (literally) after peak classes: "What worked today? One thing to improve? Class you'd love to see?" Takes 60 seconds, captures honest feedback when they're still in the zone. Forms get ignored; conversations don't.

• **Make it reward-driven immediately.** Every 5th feedback form enters a monthly draw for £20 off next month's membership or a free PT session. This drives participation from day one—you'll actually get usable data instead of 12 responses from 200 members.

• **Assign one person (you, or one trusted staff member) as the weekly synthesizer.** Every Friday, they spend 30 mins reading feedback and flagging patterns to your instructor team. Themes get discussed at weekly staff huddle. Without this *action step*, feedback dies in a pile and members notice nothing changed.

• **Publicly close the loop monthly.** Post a 1-minute video or gym notice: "You asked for..." and "We're now..." Show 2-3 specific changes you've made from their feedback. This proves the system works and quadruples next month's participation.

• **Track the conversion angle early.** Note which feedback comes from newer members (< 3 months) vs. long-term—new member input is gold for your onboarding/stickiness problems.

What's your peak class time? That's where to launch this.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Start with your actual current process, not the ideal one**
Shadow your team for 2-3 days. Document what they're *actually* doing right now for each service—not what you think should happen. This is your baseline. You'll spot where inconsistency kills member experience. Record common member questions for each service tier; these gaps become your SOP sections.

**2. Create one SOP template, then batch-build them**
Design a single template with: Pre-service (what member does before arrival), During (exact steps your staff takes), Post-service (follow-up/retention). Use this for all five services so they look/feel cohesive. This takes 2-3 hours tops instead of reinventing for each one.

**3. Assign ownership and set a hard deadline**
Pick one team member per service (the person who knows it best, not the busiest person). Give them the template + 5 days to draft their SOP. You then spend 1 hour per SOP editing for clarity and consistency. Make it tight—SOPs should be 1-2 pages max or they won't get used.

**4. Test with new members before finalizing**
Have staff use draft SOPs with 2-3 new members in each category. You'll immediately see what's missing or unclear. Refine, then lock it in. This prevents wasted effort on unusable documents.

**5. Make them live and trackable**
Store in a shared doc (Google Drive/Notion). Assign one person to audit that staff are following them quarterly. SOPs that sit in a folder get ignored—tie them to onboarding and spot-checks

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar Execution for The Forge

**1. Use a template-based batching system, not daily posting**
— Block 2 hours every other Sunday. Create 6-8 posts at once (mix: member transformations, class highlights, motivational quotes, behind-the-scenes gym life). Batch-shoot video content in one session. Use Buffer or Later to schedule everything in bulk rather than manually posting. This removes the "daily discipline" friction that kills calendars.

**2. Create a repeating 4-post weekly formula**
— Monday: Member win/transformation (builds community proof). Wednesday: Class/workout tip (drives signups). Friday: Weekend motivation or community event. Sunday: Behind-the-scenes or team spotlight. This structure removes the "what do I post?" paralysis and makes batching faster.

**3. Source content directly from members (0% extra work)**
— Ask members to film 10-15 second "before I joined/after 3 months" clips during their gym sessions. Offer a free class or branded water bottle. You get authentic testimonials without production time. Post 1-2 per week. This also increases member investment in your brand.

**4. Assign ownership to your strongest staff member with a simple brief doc**
— Don't do this yourself if someone on your team loves posting. Create a one-page "content brief" (tone, hashtags, posting times, theme days). Check-in monthly, not daily. Pay them an extra £50-100/month if needed. This scales your consistency when you're busy.

**5. Link posts to your weekly class schedule/promotions**
— If you're running a "new member week" or pushing spin classes,

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Videography Sessions with Jordan - Execution Plan

• **Lock it in NOW with a 12-month calendar invite** — Don't wing this. Send Jordan a recurring block (every 8 weeks, same day/time) with 3-4 specific content requirements per session. This removes the "what do we film?" friction that kills consistency.

• **Pre-plan 2-3 content angles per session before Jordan arrives** — Have Grace or a team member list: 1 member transformation story to capture, 1 class/training moment, 1 "behind-the-scenes" bit. Assign a staff member to brief Jordan 48 hours prior so he's not improvising on-site.

• **Designate one person as the "content quarterback"** — This person coordinates with Jordan, collects footage, and gets rough edits back within 1 week. Without a single owner, videos languish in limbo for months.

• **Tie each session to a specific launch date** — Film in Week 1 of the 8-week cycle; commit to publishing across socials/website by Week 3. This deadline discipline prevents footage from becoming stale inventory.

• **Batch-produce captions + hashtags the week videos drop** — Grace writes 3-4 caption variations per video for different platforms (Instagram Reels, TikTok, LinkedIn). Takes 20 mins if done as a batch, not scattered.

**Bottom line:** The calendar invite + content brief + one owner + hard publish date = actually getting this done.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries — Execution Plan

• **Audit the current leakage first (today)** — Pull last month's trial enquiry data. Map where they drop off: initial contact → first class → week 3 → conversion. You'll likely find 40-60% abandon between enquiry and first class. This tells you *where* automation needs muscle, not everywhere.

• **Build a 3-touch sequence, not 5** — Stop over-automating. Send: (1) Enquiry confirmation + specific class time link within 2 hours (human urgency), (2) Day-before-first-class reminder with locker/parking details (removes friction), (3) Post-class message from *staff member by name* asking how they felt. Automation kills relationships at the conversion point.

• **Lock in the "week 3 intervention"** — This is where trials stall. Auto-send a progress check + 1-1 pairing offer (intro to a regular member for accountability) on day 15. Make this manual or Zapier-triggered based on attendance. This single touch likely moves your needle 15-20%.

• **Use WhatsApp/SMS, not email** — Your demographic (women in Belfast) responds 3x faster to SMS. Set up Twilio or a tool like Klaviyo for SMS sequences. Emails go in the noise; texts feel personal and get opened within minutes.

• **Measure the actual KPI weekly** — Track trial-to-paid conversion rate by cohort (which automation sequence = which result). Start with 30 days of data. If it moves from 25% to 35%, you've

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Execute Role-Based Action Checklists for The Forge

**1. Start with output, work backwards**
Don't list generic tasks. For each person (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah), define the ONE metric they own that moves revenue/retention, then itemize only the weekly actions that directly impact it. Example: If JoJo owns member acquisition, her checklist includes "3 outreach calls to lapsed members" + "1 referral follow-up batch" — not admin tasks.

**2. Make KPIs defensible in 60 seconds**
Each role needs 2-3 KPIs max, tied to a measurable outcome. "JoJo: 5 trial bookings/week" beats "increase awareness." For Eilis (assuming operations), it might be "staff punctuality 95%+ AND class utilization ≥75%." Sarah gets clarity on her number too. They need to know if they're winning by Friday.

**3. Build the checklist as a shared Slack/Google Doc template**
Weekly recurring format: [Name] | [KPI Target] | [Mon-Fri Actions] | [Completion checkbox] | [Weekly review slot (10 mins)]. Make it visible, not hidden in a spreadsheet. Post a screenshot of the template and circulate by end of this week — no "coming soon."

**4. Lock in accountability in a 15-min huddle**
Present each person's checklist to them 1-on-1, not as a group announcement. Ask: "What do you need from me to hit this?" and "What would derail you?" Document pushback — it tells you if the target is real or if you

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before the session (48 hours prior):**
- Send each team member a 1-pager with the SSO, their specific role in the 90-day plan, and 2-3 metrics they directly own. No surprises in the room—they've already processed it.

**During the session (60 mins max):**
- Start with *why* (member outcomes, revenue target, competitive position). Spend 10 mins here—emotion + logic.
- Go person-by-person: "Here's your lane. Here's what success looks like for you. Here's where you intersect with Sarah/Emma/whoever." Make it granular—not "marketing" but "you're running 2 referral campaigns and one social push per week."
- Call out one potential bottleneck everyone will face together and solve it live (e.g., "we'll need X signups to hit target—what blocks that?"). Builds collective ownership.

**The key move:**
- End with written commitments. Each person writes down their top 3 priorities for the next 90 days and shares them aloud. 2 minutes of mild accountability beats weeks of assuming everyone's aligned.

**After:**
- Slack the recording + one-pager recap within 24 hours. Reference it in 1:1s monthly to track drift.

This isn't motivation theater—it's removing ambiguity so people can actually execute.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge

**Get the real picture in 48 hours:**
- Run a 15-min 1-on-1 with each team member asking: "What takes 80% of your mental energy?" and "What should you be doing but aren't?" Listen for resentment, not just tasks. You'll spot your bottlenecks faster than any spreadsheet.

**Map the actual workload (not job titles):**
- Create a simple table: each role down the left, every recurring task/responsibility across the top, time spent per week in each cell. You'll immediately see who's carrying three people's weight—usually it's customer-facing staff doing admin, cleaning *and* sales.

**Rebalance by removing, not just shuffling:**
- Don't just reassign tasks—ask "Can this task die?" (redundant social media posts, excessive reporting, over-complicated booking processes). Delete 2-3 tasks before moving anything. This saves time and morale more than shuffling deck chairs.

**Assign one "quick win" task per person by day 10:**
- Give someone drowning in PT admin a new responsibility they *want* (like class curation or member ambassador program). Visibility + autonomy beats complaint management.

**Lock in the new rhythm with a simple weekly standup:**
- 20 mins, every Monday—each person says what's working/breaking. Catch rebalancing drift before it solidifies back into old patterns.

What's your biggest team bottleneck right now?

# Cost Reduction Execution for The Forge

• **Audit your top 3 expense categories right now** — utilities, staff payroll, and supplier contracts almost always hide 10-15% fat. Pull 6 months of P&L, rank by spend, and you'll find your 10% in 2-3 categories, not scattered across 20. Focus there first.

• **Renegotiate supplier contracts this week** — call your equipment maintenance, cleaning supplies, and towel service providers. Tell them you're getting competitive quotes (even if you're not yet) and want their best price to keep the relationship. You'll typically get 8-12% off without switching vendors or disrupting service.

• **Cut a staffing shift, not headcount** — instead of firing someone, eliminate your lowest-revenue day shift or late evening slot. Redeploy that person to peak hours (6-8am, 5-7pm) where member retention matters most. Keeps morale intact, improves member experience, cuts costs.

• **Switch to LED lighting + programmable thermostats** — gyms waste thousands on 24/7 full lighting and climate control. Install motion sensors and drop temps 1-2 degrees during off-peak hours. Initial investment ~£1,200-1,500; payback in 6 months; 12-15% utility savings ongoing.

• **Bundle membership billing** — offer a 15% discount for annual upfront payment instead of monthly. Improves cash flow, reduces payment processing fees (currently eating 2-3%), and drops churn. This alone gets you 3-5% cost reduction without cutting a penny.

Which one starts this week?

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs — The Forge

**Make it a 90-minute Friday routine, not a wandering admin task:**
- Block 9-10:30am every Friday to pull data (bank feeds, membership software, PT booking system). Batch it once weekly so it doesn't drag across the month.

**Track the 4 metrics that actually move gym growth decisions:**
- Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR from memberships) vs. target — shows if you're losing members or growing
- Member Acquisition Cost vs. Lifetime Value — tells you if your marketing spend is profitable (if CAC > LTV, your referral/discount strategy is broken)
- Class utilization % (booked spots ÷ available spots) — unused capacity = wasted PT/class revenue potential
- Cash runway (cash balance ÷ monthly burn rate) — how many months can you survive without new income?

**Stop reporting vanity metrics:**
- Ditch "total members" or "total revenue." These hide the real story (members acquired ≠ retained; one-off PT sales ≠ sustainable income). The 4 above tell you where to act.

**Link each KPI to ONE decision it unlocks:**
- MRR trending down? → Retention crisis — audit cancellations this week.
- Class utilization <70%? → Reprogram class times or boost marketing to specific slots.
- CAC too high? → Pause paid ads; double down on member referral rewards instead.

**Set the threshold now:** Decide right now what number triggers action (e.g., "if MRR drops 5%, we pause new hires; if utilization <65%,

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Action Plan

• **Pull 12 months of actual data NOW** — membership fees, class pack sales, PT sessions, cancellations, seasonal dips. Don't forecast from assumptions. Grace likely has this in her bank statements and membership software already. Spend 2 hours max extracting it into a simple spreadsheet.

• **Build a "three scenario" model** — conservative (10% growth), realistic (25% growth based on her pipeline), aggressive (40%). Each should show monthly revenue impact and the specific activities driving it (e.g., "add 15 members at £45/month" or "launch corporate packages at £2k/month"). This forces clarity on what growth actually requires.

• **Identify the 2-3 cash constraints first** — What stops The Forge from hitting her target? Staffing capacity? Studio space? Marketing budget? Her forecast should highlight THIS bottleneck, not just pretty numbers. That's where strategy lives.

• **Link forecast to operating costs ruthlessly** — For every revenue line item, immediately add the cost impact (e.g., more members = more cleaning, utilities, staff hours). Too many gyms forecast revenue without nailing the margin collapse at scale.

• **Set a monthly review cadence** — Forecast is worthless if it's not a living document. Grace should review actual vs. forecast the first Friday of each month for 20 minutes. Adjust next month's actions based on what's tracking/missing.

What's her current monthly revenue range? That changes whether she's pre-revenue testing or optimizing an existing base.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Execute the P&L Map for The Forge

• **Start with 4 weeks of transaction data RIGHT NOW** — pull from your payment system (Stripe, Square, whatever you use) and categorize every single transaction by service type. Don't wait for perfect historical data; get messy data today. 4 weeks is enough to spot patterns.

• **Build a dead-simple spreadsheet: Revenue - Direct Costs = Contribution Margin per service** — for each (classes, PT, add-ons like merchandise/supplements), calculate what each pound of sales actually contributes after the obvious costs (instructor wages for that service, product costs). Ignore overhead allocation for now — that clouds the real picture.

• **PT sessions will look artificially profitable** — they always do. But flag the hidden truth: What % of your PT clients never upgrade to classes? What's your retention cost? That's where PT's real value is — it's your sales engine, not your cash cow. This matters for pricing strategy later.

• **Focus on the add-ons number first** — this is where Grace wins. If you're selling 200 classes/month but only 15 protein shakes, there's your growth lever. That's where margin sits. Calculate add-on revenue per class attendee, then you'll know exactly what your conversion opportunity is.

• **Use this one insight to act this week** — once you see which service actually funds growth, decide: Do you want to push volume in the high-margin service, or invest in converting more people into that service? That answer changes everything about your next move.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Automations: The Forge Action Plan

• **Run a parallel test immediately**: Pick 3-5 email addresses (use yourself, staff, trusted members) and submit enquiries through your website TODAY. Watch what they receive, when, and in what order. This exposes duplicates and broken sequences faster than reviewing backend settings. Document everything.

• **Map the actual journey, not the intended one**: Log into each automation tool you use (Mailchimp, Zapier, whatever's connected). Trace EVERY step from website form → email 1 → email 2 → SMS/call to trial booking. Duplicates hide in multiple tools firing the same message—check if Mailchimp AND Zapier are both sending welcome emails.

• **Kill the low-performers ruthlessly**: If someone gets 4 emails before their trial, cut it to 2-3 max. Women signing up for your gym don't need a novel—they need clarity on: (1) confirm receipt + next steps, (2) trial booking link, (3) one reminder 24hrs before. Measure open rates; anything under 30% gets deleted.

• **Assign ONE person ownership + a deadline**: This stalls without a named owner. Give them 5 days to complete the parallel test and document findings in a simple spreadsheet. This becomes your baseline to measure improvements against.

• **Test the conversion endpoint**: Book a trial yourself through the system. Can someone actually complete it without friction? If the booking form is clunky or the confirmation doesn't land, no automation fix matters.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Execution Plan

**Identify Your 3 Tasks (Pick These First)**
- **Email enquiry responses** — repetitive "thanks for interest, here's pricing/class schedule/trial offer" → Build a Claude/ChatGPT bot to draft these in seconds, Grace reviews & sends. Saves 5-7 hours/month.
- **New member onboarding messages** — welcome text, gym induction checklist, goal-setting questionnaire → Automate via Zapier or Make so it triggers immediately after signup, personalised with their name.
- **Social media caption/copy** — class announcements, member spotlights, promotional posts → Use Claude to generate 3-4 caption options from a simple prompt (e.g., "spinning class, energetic vibe, call-to-action") in 2 minutes flat.

**Build It This Week (Cheap & Fast)**
- Use **Zapier free tier** (3 tasks) to connect your booking system (Class Pass, MindBody, whatever you use) → Gmail/WhatsApp → Claude API. Cost: £0 to test.
- **No coding needed** — Zapier's AI action connects directly to ChatGPT; Grace writes one good prompt per task, Zapier repeats it.
- Test each automation on **5 real enquiries first**. Tweak the prompt wording based on what needs fixing (too formal? too casual? missing info?).

**Get Actual ROI (Track This)**
- Time saved per task × frequency = weekly hours freed. Grace redeploys that time to member retention calls or sales outreach — the high-value work.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Execution for The Forge Website

• **Pick ONE quick win today: SSL certificate + basic firewall.** If you're not HTTPS, Google already penalizes you + customers see "not secure" warnings. Cost: £30-100/year. Use your hosting provider's one-click SSL (most include it). Simultaneously enable their basic DDoS protection—10 minutes of clicking.

• **Audit what data you're actually holding.** Membership forms, payment details, progress photos? Map it. This determines what you legally MUST protect under GDPR. If you're taking card payments, you're liable—no shortcuts. Most gym management software (Mindbody, Mariana Tek) handle PCI compliance, but verify YOUR specific setup with them in writing.

• **Lock down admin access NOW.** Change default passwords on your hosting/CMS. Enable two-factor authentication on WordPress admin (if that's your platform). This stops 80% of breaches. Takes 30 minutes, prevents the nightmare.

• **Monthly task, not one-off: backups + monitoring.** Set automatic daily backups (your hosting likely offers this for £5-10/month). Add a basic uptime monitor (UptimeRobot, free version) so you know if the site goes down before customers tell you.

• **Skip the £5k security audit for now.** Instead, run a free Sucuri or Wordfence scan monthly. When revenue justifies it (say, 500+ paying members), invest in a proper penetration test.

**What's the current state of your website?** Knowing your platform (WordPress/Squarespace/custom build) + payment processor changes the priority order
